text: All wisdom is from the Lord God, and hath been always with him, and is before all time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who hath numbered the sand of the sea, and the drops of rain, and the days of the world? Who hath measured the height of heaven, and the breadth of the earth, and the depth of the abyss?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who hath searched out the wisdom of God that goeth before all things?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom hath been created before all things, and the understanding of prudence from everlasting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The word of God on high is the fountain of wisdom, and her ways are everlasting commandments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To whom hath the root of wisdom been revealed, and who hath known her wise counsels?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To whom hath the discipline of wisdom been revealed and made manifest? and who hath understood the multiplicity of her steps?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is one most high Creator Almighty, and a powerful king, and greatly to be feared, who sitteth upon his throne, and is the God of dominion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He created her in the Holy Ghost, and saw her, and numbered her, and measured her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he poured her out upon all his works, and upon all flesh according to his gift, and hath given her to them that love him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord is honour, and glory, and gladness, and a crown of joy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord shall delight the heart, and shall give joy, and gladness, and length of days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With him that feareth the Lord, it shall go well in the latter end, and in the day of his death he shall be blessed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The love of God is honourable wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they to whom she shall shew herself love her by the sight, and by the knowledge of her great works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom, and was created with the faithful in the womb, it walketh with chosen women, and is known with the just and faithful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord is the religiousness of knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Religiousness shall keep and justify the heart, it shall give joy and gladness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It shall go well with him that feareth the Lord, and in the days of his end he shall be blessed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To fear God is the fulness of wisdom, and fulness is from the fruits thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She shall fill all her house with her increase, and the storehouses with her treasures.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord is a crown of wisdom, filling up peace and the fruit of salvation:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it hath seen, and numbered her: but both are the gifts of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom shall distribute knowledge, and understanding of prudence: and exalteth the glory of them that hold her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The root of wisdom is to fear the Lord: and the branches thereof are long-lived.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the treasures of wisdom is understanding, and religiousness of knowledge: but to sinners wisdom is an abomination.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of the Lord driveth out sin:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he that is without fear, cannot be justified: for the wrath of his high spirits is his ruin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A patient man shall bear for a time, and afterwards joy shall be restored to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A good understanding will hide his words for a time, and the lips of many shall declare his wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the treasures of wisdom is the signification of discipline:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the worship of God is an abomination to a sinner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Son, if thou desire wisdom, keep justice, and God will give her to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the fear of the Lord is wisdom and discipline: and that which is agreeable to him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Is faith, and meekness: and he will fill up his treasures.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not incredulous to the fear of the Lord: and come not to him with a double heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not a hypocrite in the sight of men, and let not thy lips be a stumblingblock to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Watch over them, lest thou fall, and bring dishonour upon thy soul,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God discover thy secrets, and cast thee down in the midst of the congregation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because thou camest to the Lord wickedly, and thy heart is full of guile and deceit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Son, when thou comest to the service of God, stand in justice and in fear, and prepare thy soul for temptation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Humble thy heart, and endure: incline thy ear, and receive the words of understanding: and make not haste in the time of clouds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wait on God with patience: join thyself to God, and endure, that thy life may be increased in the latter end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take all that shall be brought upon thee: and in thy sorrow endure, and in thy humiliation keep patience.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For gold and silver are tried in the fire, but acceptable men in the furnace of humiliation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Believe God, and he will recover thee: and direct thy way, and trust in him. Keep his fear, and grow old therein.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Ye that fear the Lord, wait for his mercy: and go not aside from him lest ye fall.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Ye that fear the Lord, believe him: and your reward shall not be made void.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Ye that fear the Lord hope in him, and mercy shall come to you for your delight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Ye that fear the Lord, love him, and your hearts shall be enlightened.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My children behold the generations of men: and know ye that no one hath hoped in the Lord, and hath been confounded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For who hath continued in his commandment, and hath been forsaken? or who hath called upon him, and he despised him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For God is compassionate and merciful, and will forgive sins in the day of tribulation: and he is a protector to all that seek him in truth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Woe to them that are of a double heart and to wicked lips, and to the hands that do evil, and to the sinner that goeth on the earth two ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Woe to them that are fainthearted, who believe not God: and therefore they shall not be protected by him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Woe to them that have lost patience, and that have forsaken the right ways, and have gone aside into crooked ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And what will they do, when the Lord shall begin to examine?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that fear the Lord, will not be incredulous to his word: and they that love him, will keep his way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that fear the Lord, will seek after the things that are well pleasing to him: and they that love him, shall be filled with his law.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that fear the Lord, will prepare their hearts, and in his sight will sanctify their souls,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that fear the Lord, keep his commandments, and will have patience even until his visitation,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Saying: If we do not penance, we shall fall into the hands of the Lord, and not into the hands of men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For according to his greatness, so also is his mercy with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of wisdom are the church of the just: and their generation, obedience and love.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Children, hear the judgment of your father, and so do that you may be saved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For God hath made the father honourable to the children: and seeking the judgment of the mothers, hath confirmed it upon the children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that loveth God, shall obtain pardon for his sins by prayer, and shall refrain himself from them, and shall be heard in the prayer of days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he that honoureth his mother is as one that layeth up a treasure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that honoureth his father shall have joy in his own children, and in the day of his prayer he shall be heard.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that honoureth his father shall enjoy a long life: and he that obeyeth the father, shall be a comfort to his mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that feareth the Lord, honoureth his parents, and will serve them as his masters that brought him into the world.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Honour thy father, in work and word, and all patience,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That a blessing may come upon thee from him, and his blessing may remain in the latter end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The father’s blessing establisheth the houses of the children: but the mother’s curse rooteth up the foundation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Glory not in the dishonour of thy father: for his shame is no glory to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the glory of a man is from the honour of his father, and a father without honour is the disgrace of the son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Son, support the old age of thy father, and grieve him not in his life;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if his understanding fail, have patience with him, and despise him not when thou art in thy strength: for the relieving of the father shall not be forgotten.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For good shall be repaid to thee for the sin of thy mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in justice thou shalt be built up, and in the day of affliction thou shalt be remembered: and thy sins shall melt away as the ice in the fair warm weather.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Of what an evil fame is he that forsaketh his father: and he is cursed of God that angereth his mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, do thy works in meekness, and thou shalt be beloved above the glory of men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The greater thou art, the more humble thyself in all things, and thou shalt find grace before God:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For great is the power of God alone, and he is honoured by the humble.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Seek not the things that are too high for thee, and search not into things above thy ability: but the things that God hath commanded thee, think on them always, and in many of his works be not curious.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it is not necessary for thee to see with thy eyes those things that are hid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In unnecessary matters be not over curious, and in many of his works thou shalt not be inquisitive.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For many things are shewn to thee above the understanding of men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the suspicion of them hath deceived many, and hath detained their minds in vanity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A hard heart shall fear evil at the last: and he that loveth danger shall perish in it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A heart that goeth two ways shall not have success, and the perverse of heart shall be scandalized therein.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wicked heart shall be laden with sorrows, and the sinner will add sin to sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The congregation of the proud shall not be healed: for the plant of wickedness shall take root in them, and it shall not be perceived.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of the wise is understood in wisdom, and a good ear will hear wisdom with all desire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise heart, and which hath understanding, will abstain from sins, and in the works of justice shall have success.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Water quencheth a flaming fire, and alms resisteth sins:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God provideth for him that sheweth favour: he remembereth him afterwards, and in the time of his fall he shall find a sure stay.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Son, defraud not the poor of alms, and turn not away thy eyes from the poor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Despise not the hungry soul: and provoke not the poor in his want.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Afflict not the heart of the needy, and defer not to give to him that is in distress.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Reject not the petition of the afflicted: and turn not away thy face from the needy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Turn not away thy eyes from the poor for fear of anger: and leave not to them that ask of thee to curse thee behind thy back.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the prayer of him that curseth thee in the bitterness of his soul, shall be heard, for he that made him will hear him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Make thyself affable to the congregation of the poor, and humble thy soul to the ancient, and bow thy head to a great man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Bow down thy ear cheerfully to the poor, and pay what thou owest, and answer him peaceable words with mildness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Deliver him that suffereth wrong out of the hand of the proud: and be not fainthearted in thy soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In judging be merciful to the fatherless as a father, and as a husband to their mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt be as the obedient son of the most High, and he will have mercy on thee more than a mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom inspireth life into her children, and protecteth them that seek after her, and will go before them in the way of justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he that loveth her, loveth life: and they that watch for her, shall embrace her sweetness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that hold her fast, shall inherit life: and whithersoever she entereth, God will give a blessing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that serve her, shall be servants to the holy one: and God loveth them that love her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hearkeneth to her, shall judge nations: and he that looketh upon her, shall remain secure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he trust to her, he shall inherit her, and his generation shall be in assurance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For she walketh with him in temptation, and at the first she chooseth him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She will bring upon him fear and dread and trial: and she will scourge him with the affliction of her discipline, till she try him by her laws, and trust his soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then she will strengthen him, and make a straight way to him, and give him joy,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And will disclose her secrets to him, and will heap upon him treasures of knowledge and understanding of justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if he go astray, she will forsake him, and deliver him into the hands of his enemy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Son, observe the time, and fly from evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thy soul be not ashamed to say the truth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For there is a shame that bringeth sin, and there is a shame that bringeth glory and grace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Accept no person against thy own person, nor against thy soul a lie.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Reverence not thy neighbour in his fall:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And refrain not to speak in the time of salvation. Hide not thy wisdom in her beauty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For by the tongue wisdom is discerned: and understanding, and knowledge, and learning by the word of the wise, and steadfastness in the works of justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In nowise speak against the truth, but be ashamed of the lie of thy ignorance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not ashamed to confess thy sins, but submit not thyself to every man for sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Resist not against the face of the mighty, and do not strive against the stream of the river.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Strive for justice for thy soul, and even unto death fight for justice, and God will overthrow thy enemies for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not hasty in thy tongue: and slack and remiss in thy works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not as a lion in thy house, terrifying them of thy household, and oppressing them that are under thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let not thy hand be stretched out to receive, and shut when thou shouldst give.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Set not thy heart upon unjust possessions, and say not: I have enough to live on: for it shall be of no service in the time of vengeance and darkness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Follow not in thy strength the desires of thy heart:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And say not: How mighty am I? and who shall bring me under for my deeds? for God will surely take revenge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: I have sinned, and what harm hath befallen me? for the most High is a patient rewarder.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not without fear about sin forgiven, and add not sin upon sin:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And say not: The mercy of the Lord is great, he will have mercy on the multitude of my sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For mercy and wrath quickly come from him, and his wrath looketh upon sinners.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Delay not to be converted to the Lord, and defer it not from day to day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For his wrath shall come on a sudden, and in the time of vengeance he will destroy thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not anxious for goods unjustly gotten: for they shall not profit thee in the day of calamity and revenge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Winnow not with every wind, and go not into every way: for so is every sinner proved by a double tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be steadfast in the way of the Lord, and in the truth of thy judgment, and in knowledge, and let the word of peace and justice keep with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be meek to hear the word, that thou mayst understand: and return a true answer with wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou have understanding, answer thy neighbour: but if not, let thy hand be upon thy mouth, lest thou be surprised in an unskilful word, and be confounded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Honour and glory is in the word of the wise, but the tongue of the fool is his ruin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not called a whisperer, and be not taken in thy tongue, and confounded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For confusion and repentance is upon a thief, and an evil mark of disgrace upon the double tongued, but to the whisperer hatred, and enmity, and reproach.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Justify alike the small and the great.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Instead of a friend become not an enemy to thy neighbour: for an evil man shall inherit reproach and shame, so shall every sinner that is envious and double tongued.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Extol not thyself in the thoughts of thy soul like a bull: lest thy strength be quashed by folly,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it eat up thy leaves, and destroy thy fruit, and thou be left as a dry tree in the wilderness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For a wicked soul shall destroy him that hath it, and maketh him to be a joy to his enemies, and shall lead him into the lot of the wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A sweet word multiplieth friends, and appeaseth enemies, and a gracious tongue in a good man aboundeth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be in peace with many, but let one of a thousand be thy counsellor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou wouldst get a friend, try him before thou takest him, and do not credit him easily.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For there is a friend for his own occasion, and he will not abide in the day of thy trouble.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there is a friend that turneth to enmity; and there is a friend that will disclose hatred and strife and reproaches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there is a friend a companion at the table, and he will not abide in the day of distress.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A friend if he continue steadfast, shall be to thee as thyself, and shall act with confidence among them of thy household.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he humble himself before thee, and hide himself from thy face, thou shalt have unanimous friendship for good.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Separate thyself from thy enemies, and take heed of thy friends.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A faithful friend is a strong defence: and he that hath found him, hath found a treasure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nothing can be compared to a faithful friend, and no weight of gold and silver is able to countervail the goodness of his fidelity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A faithful friend is the medicine of life and immortality: and they that fear the Lord, shall find him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that feareth God, shall likewise have good friendship: because according to him shall his friend be.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, from thy youth up receive instruction, and even to thy grey hairs thou shalt find wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come to her as one that plougheth, and soweth, and wait for her good fruits:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For in working about her thou shalt labour a little, and shalt quickly eat of her fruits.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How very unpleasant is wisdom to the unlearned, and the unwise will not continue with her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She shall be to them as a mighty stone of trial, and they will cast her from them before it be long.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the wisdom of doctrine is according to her name, and she is not manifest unto many, but with them to whom she is known, she continueth even to the sight of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give ear, my son, and take wise counsel, and cast not away my advice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Put thy feet into her fetters, and thy neck into her chains:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Bow down thy shoulder, and bear her, and be not grieved with her bands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come to her with all thy mind, and keep her ways with all thy power.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Search for her, and she shall be made known to thee, and when thou hast gotten her, let her not go:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For in the latter end thou shalt find rest in her, and she shall be turned to thy joy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then shall her fetters be a strong defence for thee, and a firm foundation, and her chain a robe of glory:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For in her is the beauty of life, and her bands are a healthful binding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt put her on as a robe of glory, and thou shalt set her upon thee as a crown of joy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, if thou wilt attend to me, thou shalt learn: and if thou wilt apply thy mind, thou shalt be wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou wilt incline thy ear, thou shalt receive instruction: and if thou love to hear, thou shalt be wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Stand in the multitude of ancients that are wise, and join thyself from thy heart to their wisdom, that thou mayst hear every discourse of God, and the sayings of praise may not escape thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if thou see a man of understanding, go to him early in the morning, and let thy foot wear the steps of his doors.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let thy thoughts be upon the precepts of God, and meditate continually on his commandments: and he will give thee a heart, and the desire of wisdom shall be given to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do no evils, and no evils shall lay hold of thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Depart from the unjust, and evils shall depart from thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, sow not evils in the furrows of injustice, and thou shalt not reap them sevenfold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Seek not of the Lord a preeminence, nor of the king the seat of honour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Justify not thyself before God, for he knoweth the heart: and desire not to appear wise before the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Seek not to be made a judge, unless thou have strength enough to extirpate iniquities: lest thou fear the person of the powerful, and lay a stumblingblock for thy integrity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Offend not against the multitude of a city, neither cast thyself in upon the people,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nor bind sin to sin: for even in one thou shalt not be unpunished.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not fainthearted in thy mind:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neglect not to pray, and to give alms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: God will have respect to the multitude of my gifts, and when I offer to the most high God, he will accept my offerings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Laugh no man to scorn in the bitterness of his soul: for there is one that humbleth and exalteth, God who seeth all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Devise not a lie against thy brother: neither do the like against thy friend.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not willing to make any manner of lie: for the custom thereof is not good.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not full of words in a multitude of ancients, and repeat not the word in thy prayer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hate not laborious works, nor husbandry ordained by the most High.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Number not thyself among the multitude of the disorderly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember wrath, for it will not tarry long.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Humble thy spirit very much: for the vengeance on the flesh of the ungodly is fire and worms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do not transgress against thy friend deferring money, nor despise thy dear brother for the sake of gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Depart not from a wise and good wife, whom thou hast gotten in the fear of the Lord: for the grace of her modesty is above gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hurt not the servant that worketh faithfully, nor the hired man that giveth thee his life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let a wise servant be dear to thee as thy own soul, defraud him not of liberty, nor leave him needy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou cattle? have an eye to them: and if they be for thy profit, keep them with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou children? instruct them, and bow down their neck from their childhood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou daughters? have a care of their body, and shew not thy countenance gay towards them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Marry thy daughter well, and thou shalt do a great work, and give her to a wise man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou hast a wife according to thy soul, cast her not off: and to her that is hateful, trust not thyself. With thy whole heart,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Honour thy father, and forget not the groanings of thy mother:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember that thou hadst not been born but through them: and make a return to them as they have done for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With all thy soul fear the Lord, and reverence his priests.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With all thy strength love him that made thee: and forsake not his ministers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Honour God with all thy soul and give honour to the priests, and purify thyself with thy arms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give them their portion, as it is commanded thee, of the firstfruits and of purifications: and for thy negligences purify thyself with a few.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Offer to the Lord the gift of thy shoulders, and the sacrifice of sanctification, and the firstfruits of the holy things:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And stretch out thy hand to the poor, that thy expiation and thy blessing may be perfected.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A gift hath grace in the sight of all the living, and restrain not grace from the dead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not wanting in comforting them that weep, and walk with them that mourn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not slow to visit the sick: for by these things thou shalt be confirmed in love.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In all thy works remember thy last end, and thou shalt never sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Strive not with a powerful man, lest thou fall into his hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Contend not with a rich man, lest he bring an action against thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For gold and silver hath destroyed many, and hath reached even to the heart of kings, and perverted them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Strive not with a man that is full of tongue, and heap not wood upon his fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Communicate not with an ignorant man, lest he speak ill of thy family.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Despise not a man that turneth away from sin, nor reproach him therewith: remember that we are all worthy of reproof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Despise not a man in his old age; for we also shall become old.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Rejoice not at the death of thy enemy; knowing that we all die, and are not willing that others should rejoice at our death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Despise not the discourse of them that are ancient and wise, but acquaint thyself with their proverbs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For of them thou shalt learn wisdom, and instruction of understanding, and to serve great men without blame.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let not the discourse of the ancients escape thee, for they have learned of their fathers:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For of them thou shalt learn understanding, and to give an answer in time of need.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Kindle not the coals of sinners by rebuking them, lest thou be burnt with the flame of the fire of their sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Stand not against the face of an injurious person, lest he sit as a spy to entrap thee in thy words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lend not to a man that is mightier than thyself: and if thou lendest, count it as lost.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not surety above thy power: and if thou be surety, think as if thou wert to pay it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Judge not against a judge: for he judgeth according to that which is just.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go not on the way with a bold man, lest he burden thee with his evils: for he goeth according to his own will, and thou shalt perish together with his folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Quarrel not with a passionate man, and go not into the desert with a bold man: for blood is as nothing in his sight, and where there is no help he will overthrow thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Advise not with fools, for they cannot love but such things as please them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before a stranger do no matter of counsel: for thou knowest not what he will bring forth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Open not thy heart to every man: lest he repay thee with an evil turn, and speak reproachfully to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not jealous over the wife of thy bosom, lest she shew in thy regard the malice of a wicked lesson.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give not the power of thy soul to a woman, lest she enter upon thy strength, and thou be confounded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Look not upon a woman that hath a mind for many: lest thou fall into her snares.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Use not much the company of her that is a dancer, and hearken not to her, lest thou perish by the force of her charms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Gaze not upon a maiden, lest her beauty be a stumblingblock to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give not thy soul to harlots in any point: lest thou destroy thyself and thy inheritance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Look not round about thee in the ways of the city, nor wander up and down in the streets thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Turn away thy face from a woman dressed up, and gaze not about upon another’s beauty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For many have perished by the beauty of a woman, and hereby lust is enkindled as a fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Every woman that is a harlot, shall be trodden upon as dung in the way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many by admiring the beauty of another man’s wife, have become reprobate, for her conversation burneth as fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sit not at all with another man’s wife, nor repose upon the bed with her:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And strive not with her over wine, lest thy heart decline towards her and by thy blood thou fall into destruction.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forsake not an old friend, for the new will not be like to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A new friend is as new wine: it shall grow old, and thou shalt drink it with pleasure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Envy not the glory and riches of a sinner: for thou knowest not what his ruin shall be.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not pleased with the wrong done by the unjust, knowing that even to hell the wicked shall not please.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Keep thee far from the man that hath power to kill, so thou shalt not suspect the fear of death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if thou come to him, commit no fault, lest he take away thy life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Know it to be a communication with death: for thou art going in the midst of snares, and walking upon the arms of them that are grieved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: According to thy power beware of thy neighbour, and treat with the wise and prudent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let just men be thy guests, and let thy glory be in the fear of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let the thought of God be in thy mind, and all thy discourse on the commandments of the Highest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Works shall be praised for the hand of the artificers, and the prince of the people for the wisdom of his speech, but the word of the ancients for the sense.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man full of tongue is terrible in his city, and he that is rash in his word shall be hateful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise judge shall judge his people, and the government of a prudent man shall be steady.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the judge of the people is himself, so also are his ministers: and what manner of man the ruler of a city is, such also are they that dwell therein.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: An unwise king shall be the ruin of his people: and cities shall be inhabited through the prudence of the rulers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The power of the earth is in the hand of God, and in his time he will raise up a profitable ruler over it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The prosperity of man is in the hand of God, and upon the person of the scribe he shall lay his honour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember not any injury done thee by thy neighbour, and do thou nothing by deeds of injury.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Pride is hateful before God and men: and all iniquity of nations is execrable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A kingdom is translated from one people to another, because of injustices, and wrongs, and injuries, and divers deceits.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But nothing is more wicked than the covetous man. Why is earth, and ashes proud?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is not a more wicked thing than to love money: for such a one setteth even his own soul to sale: because while he liveth he hath cast away his bowels.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All power is of short life. A long sickness is troublesome to the physician.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The physician cutteth off a short sickness: so also a king is to day, and to morrow he shall die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when a man shall die, he shall inherit serpents, and beasts, and worms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The beginning of the pride of man, is to fall off from God:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because his heart is departed from him that made him: for pride is the beginning of all sin: he that holdeth it, shall be filled with maledictions, and it shall ruin him in the end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore hath the Lord disgraced the assemblies of the wicked, and hath utterly destroyed them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God hath overturned the thrones of proud princes, and hath set up the meek in their stead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God hath made the roots of proud nations to wither, and hath planted the humble of these nations.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord hath overthrown the lands of the Gentiles, and hath destroyed them even to the foundation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath made some of them to wither away, and hath destroyed them, and hath made the memory of them to cease from the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God hath abolished the memory of the proud, and hath preserved the memory of them that are humble in mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Pride was not made for men: nor wrath for the race of women.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That seed of men shall be honoured, which feareth God: but that seed shall be dishonoured, which transgresseth the commandments of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the midst of brethren their chief is honourable: so shall they that fear the Lord, be in his eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of God is the glory of the rich, and of the honourable, and of the poor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Despise not a just man that is poor, and do not magnify a sinful man that is rich.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The great man, and the judge, and the mighty is in honour: and there is none greater than he that feareth God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that are free shall serve a servant that is wise: and a man that is prudent and well instructed will not murmur when he is reproved; and he that is ignorant, shall not be honoured.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Extol not thyself in doing thy work, and linger not in the time of distress;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is he that laboureth, and aboundeth in all things, than he that boasteth himself and wanteth bread.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, keep thy soul in meekness, and give it honour according to its desert.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who will justify him that sinneth against his own soul? and who will honour him that dishonoureth his own soul?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The poor man is glorified by his discipline and fear, and there is a man that is honoured for his wealth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he that is glorified in poverty, how much more in wealth? and he that is glorified in wealth, let him fear poverty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wisdom of the humble shall exalt his head, and shall make him sit in the midst of great men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Praise not a man for his beauty, neither despise a man for his look.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The bee is small among flying things but her fruit hath the chiefest sweetness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Glory not in apparel at any time, and be not exalted in the day of thy honour: for the works of the Highest only are wonderful, and his works are glorious, and secret, and hidden.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many tyrants have sat on the throne, and he whom no man would think on, hath worn the crown.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many mighty men have been greatly brought down, and the glorious have been delivered into the hand of others.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before thou inquire, blame no man: and when thou hast inquired, reprove justly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before thou hear, answer not a word: and interrupt not others in the midst of their discourse.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Strive not in a matter which doth not concern thee, and sit not in judgment with sinners.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, meddle not with many matters: and if thou be rich, thou shalt not be free from sin: for if thou pursue after thou shalt not overtake; and if thou run before thou shalt not escape.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is an ungodly man that laboureth, and maketh haste, and is in sorrow, and is so much the more in want.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Again, there is an inactive man that wanteth help, is very weak in ability, and full of poverty:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yet the eye of God hath looked upon him for good, and hath lifted him up from his low estate, and hath exalted his head: and many have wondered at him, and have glorified God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Good things and evil, life and death, poverty and riches, are from God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom and discipline, and the knowledge of the law are with God. Love and the ways of good things are with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Error and darkness are created with sinners: and they that glory in evil things, grow old in evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The gift of God abideth with the just, and his advancement shall have success for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is one that is enriched by living sparingly, and this is the portion of his reward.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In that he saith: I have found me rest, and now I will eat of my goods alone:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he knoweth not what time shall pass, and that death approacheth, and that he must leave all to others, and shall die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be steadfast in thy covenant, and be conversant therein, and grow old in the work of thy commandments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Abide not in the works of sinners. But trust in God, and stay in thy place,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it is easy in the eyes of God on a sudden to make the poor man rich.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The blessing of God maketh haste to reward the just, and in a swift hour his blessing beareth fruit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: What need I, and what good shall I have by this?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: I am sufficient for myself: and what shall I be made worse by this?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the day of good things be not unmindful of evils: and in the day of evils be not unmindful of good things:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it is easy before God in the day of death to reward every one according to his ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The affliction of an hour maketh one forget great delights, and in the end of a man is the disclosing of his works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Praise not any man before death, for a man is known by his children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Bring not every man into thy house: for many are the snares of the deceitful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as corrupted bowels send forth stinking breath, and as the partridge is brought into the cage, and as the roe into the snare: so also is the heart of the proud, and as a spy that looketh on the fall of his neighbour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he lieth in wait and turneth good into evil, and on the elect he will lay a blot.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Of one spark cometh a great fire, and of one deceitful man much blood: and a sinful man lieth in wait for blood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take heed to thyself of a mischievous man, for he worketh evils: lest he bring upon thee reproach for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Receive a stranger in, and he shall overthrow thee with a whirlwind, and shall turn thee out of thy own.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou do good, know to whom thou dost it, and there shall be much thanks for thy good deeds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do good to the just, and thou shalt find great recompense: and if not of him, assuredly of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For there is no good for him that is always occupied in evil, and that giveth no alms: for the Highest hateth sinners, and hath mercy on the penitent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give to the merciful and uphold not the sinner: God will repay vengeance to the ungodly and to sinners, and keep them against the day of vengeance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give to the good, and receive not a sinner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do good to the humble, and give not to the ungodly: hold back thy bread, and give it not to him, lest thereby he overmaster thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou shalt receive twice as much evil for all the good thou shalt have done to him: for the Highest also hateth sinners, and will repay vengeance to the ungodly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A friend shall not be known in prosperity, and an enemy shall not be hidden in adversity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the prosperity of a man, his enemies are grieved: and a friend is known in his adversity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Never trust thy enemy for as a brass pot his wickedness rusteth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Though he humble himself and go crouching, yet take good heed and beware of him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Set him not by thee, neither let him sit on thy right hand, lest he turn into thy place, and seek to take thy seat and at the last thou acknowledge my words, and be pricked with my sayings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who will pity an enchanter struck by a serpent, or any that come near wild beasts? so is it with him that keepeth company with a wicked man, and is involved in his sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For an hour he will abide with thee: but if thou begin to decline, he will not endure it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: An enemy speaketh sweetly with his lips, but in his heart he lieth in wait, to throw thee into a pit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: An enemy weepeth with his eyes: but if he find an opportunity he will not be satisfied with blood:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if evils come upon thee, thou shalt find him there first.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: An enemy hath tears in his eyes, and while he pretendeth to help thee, will undermine thy feet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He will shake his head, and clap his hands, and whisper much, and change his countenance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that toucheth pitch, shall be defiled with it: and he that hath fellowship with the proud, shall put on pride.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall take a burden upon him that hath fellowship with one more honourable than himself. And have no fellowship with one that is richer than thyself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What agreement shall the earthen pot have with the kettle? for if they knock one against the other, it shall be broken.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The rich man hath done wrong, and yet he will fume: but the poor is wronged and must hold his peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou give, he will make use of thee: and if thou have nothing, he will forsake thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou have any thing, he will live with thee, and will make thee bare, and he will not be sorry for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he have need of thee he will deceive thee, and smiling upon thee will put thee in hope; he will speak thee fair, and will say: What wantest thou?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he will shame thee by his meats, till he have drawn thee dry twice or thrice, and at last he will laugh at thee: and afterward when he seeth thee, he will forsake thee, and shake his head at thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Humble thyself to God, and wait for his hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Beware that thou be not deceived into folly, and be humbled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not lowly in thy wisdom, lest being humbled thou be deceived into folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou be invited by one that is mightier, withdraw thyself: for so he will invite thee the more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not troublesome to him, lest thou be put back: and keep not far from him, lest thou be forgotten.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Affect not to speak with him as an equal, and believe not his many words: for by much talk he will sift thee, and smiling will examine thee concerning thy secrets.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His cruel mind will lay up thy words: and he will not spare to do thee hurt, and to cast thee into prison.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take heed to thyself, and attend diligently to what thou hearest: for thou walkest in danger of thy ruin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When thou hearest those things, see as it were in sleep, and thou shalt awake.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Love God all thy life, and call upon him for thy salvation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Every beast loveth its like: so also every man him that is nearest to himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All flesh shall consort with the like to itself, and every man shall associate himself to his like.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If the wolf shall at any time have fellowship with the lamb, so the sinner with the just.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What fellowship hath a holy man with a dog, or what part hath the rich with the poor?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wild ass is the lion’s prey in the desert: so also the poor are devoured by the rich.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as humility is an abomination to the proud: so also the rich man abhorreth the poor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When a rich man is shaken, he is kept up by his friends: but when a poor man is fallen down, he is thrust away even by his acquaintance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When a rich man hath been deceived, he hath many helpers: he hath spoken proud things, and they have justified him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The poor man was deceived, and he is rebuked also: he hath spoken wisely, and could have no place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The rich man spoke, and all held their peace, and what he said they extol even to the clouds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The poor man spoke, and they say: Who is this? and if he stumble, they will overthrow him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Riches are good to him that hath no sin in his conscience: and poverty is very wicked in the mouth of the ungodly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of a man changeth his countenance, either for good, or for evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The token of a good heart, and a good countenance thou shalt hardly find, and with labour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is the man that hath not slipped by a word out of his mouth, and is not pricked with the remorse of sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Happy is he that hath had no sadness of his mind, and who is not fallen from his hope.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Riches are not comely for a covetous man and a niggard, and what should an envious man do with gold?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that gathereth together by wronging his own soul, gathereth for others, and another will squander away his goods in rioting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is evil to himself, to whom will he be good? and he shall not take pleasure in his goods.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is none worse than he that envieth himself, and this is the reward of his wickedness:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if he do good, he doth it ignorantly, and unwillingly: and at the last he discovereth his wickedness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The eye of the envious is wicked: and he turneth away his face, and despiseth his own soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The eye of the covetous man is insatiable in his portion of iniquity: he will not be satisfied till he consume his own soul, drying it up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: An evil eye is towards evil things: and he shall not have his fill of bread, but shall be needy and pensive at his own table.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, if thou have any thing, do good to thyself, and offer to God worthy offerings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember that death is not slow, and that the covenant of hell hath been shewn to thee: for the covenant of this world shall surely die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do good to thy friend before thou die, and according to thy ability, stretching out thy hand give to the poor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Defraud not thyself of the good day, and let not the part of a good gift overpass thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
