text: Shalt thou not leave to others to divide by lot thy sorrows and labours?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give and take, and justify thy soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before thy death work justice: for in hell there is no finding food.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All flesh shall fade as grass, and as the leaf that springeth out on a green tree.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Some grow, and some fall off: so is the generation of flesh and blood, one cometh to an end, and another is born.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Every work that is corruptible shall fail in the end: and the worker thereof shall go with it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And every excellent work shall be justified: and the worker thereof shall be honoured therein.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is the man that shall continue in wisdom, and that shall meditate in his justice, and in his mind shall think of the all seeing eye of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that considereth her ways in his heart, and hath understanding in her secrets, who goeth after her as one that traceth, and stayeth in her ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He who looketh in at her windows, and hearkeneth at her door.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that lodgeth near her house, and fastening a pin in her walls shall set up his tent high unto her, where good things shall rest in his lodging for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall set his children under her shelter, and shall lodge under her branches:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall be protected under her covering from the heat, and shall rest in her glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that feareth God, will do good: and he that possesseth justice, shall lay hold on her,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she will meet him as an honourable mother, and will receive him as a wife married of a virgin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With the bread of life and understanding, she shall feed him, and give him the water of wholesome wisdom to drink: and she shall be made strong in him, and he shall not be moved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she shall hold him fast, and he shall not be confounded: and she shall exalt him among his neighbours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in the midst of the church she shall open his mouth, and shall fill him with the spirit of wisdom and understanding, and shall clothe him with a robe of glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She shall heap upon him a treasure of joy and gladness, and shall cause him to inherit an everlasting name.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But foolish men shall not obtain her, and wise men shall meet her, foolish men shall not see her: for she is far from pride and deceit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lying men shall be mindful of her: but men that speak truth shall be found with her, and shall advance, even till they come to the sight of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Praise is not seemly in the mouth of a sinner:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For wisdom came forth from God: for praise shall be with the wisdom of God, and shall abound in a faithful mouth, and the sovereign Lord will give praise unto it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: It is through God, that she is not with me: for do not thou the things that he hateth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: He hath caused me to err: for he hath no need of wicked men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord hateth all abomination of error, and they that fear him shall not love it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God made man from the beginning, and left him in the hand of his own counsel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He added his commandments and precepts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou wilt keep the commandments and perform acceptable fidelity for ever, they shall preserve thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath set water and fire before thee: stretch forth thy hand to which thou wilt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before man is life and death, good and evil, that which he shall choose shall be given him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the wisdom of God is great, and he is strong in power, seeing all men without ceasing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The eyes of the Lord are towards them that fear him, and he knoweth al the work of man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath commanded no man to do wickedly, and he hath given no man license to sin;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he desireth not a multitude of faithless and unprofitable children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Rejoice not in ungodly children, if they be multiplied: neither be delighted in them, if the fear of God be not with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Trust not to their life, and respect not their labours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For better is one that feareth God, than a thousand ungodly children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it is better to die without children, than to leave ungodly children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By one that is wise a country shall be inhabited, the tribe of the ungodly shall become desolate.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many such things hath my eyes seen, and greater things than these my ear hath heard.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the congregation of sinners a fire shall be kindled, and in an unbelieving nation wrath shall flame out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The ancient giants did not obtain pardon for their sins, who were destroyed trusting to their own strength:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he spared not the place where Lot sojourned, but abhorred them for the pride of their word.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He had not pity on them, destroying the whole nation that extolled themselves in their sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So did he with the six hundred thousand footmen, who were gathered together in the hardness of their heart: and if one had been stiffnecked, it is a wonder if he had escaped unpunished:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For mercy and wrath are with him. He is mighty to forgive, and to pour out indignation:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: According as his mercy is, so his correction judgeth a man according to his works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sinner shall not escape in his rapines, and the patience of him that sheweth mercy shall not be put off.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All mercy shall make a place for every man according to the merit of his works, and according to the wisdom of his sojournment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: I shall be hidden from God, and who shall remember me from on high?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In such a multitude I shall not be known: for what is my soul in such an immense creation?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold the heaven, and the heavens of heavens, the deep, and all the earth, and the things that are in them, shall be moved in his sight,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mountains also, and the hills, and the foundations of the earth: when God shall look upon them, they shall be shaken with trembling.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in all these things the heart is senseless: and every heart is understood by him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his ways who shall understand, and the storm, which no eye of man shall see?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For many of his works are hidden, but the works of his justice who shall declare? or who shall endure? for the testament is far from some, and the examination of all is in the end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that wanteth understanding thinketh vain things, and the foolish, and erring man, thinketh foolish things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hearken to me, my son, and learn the discipline of understanding, and attend to my words in thy heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I will shew forth good doctrine in equity, and will seek to declare wisdom: and attend to my words in thy heart, whilst with equity of spirit I tell thee the virtues that God hath put upon his works from the beginning, and I shew forth in truth his knowledge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The works of God are done in judgment from the beginning, and from the making of them he distinguished their parts, and their beginnings in their generations.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He beautified their works for ever, they have neither hungered, nor laboured, and they have not ceased from their works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nor shall any of them straiten his neighbour at any time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not thou incredulous to his word.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this God looked upon the earth, and filled it with his goods.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The soul of every living thing hath shewn forth before the face thereof, and into it they return again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God created man of the earth, and made him after his own image.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he turned him into it again, and clothed him with strength according to himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He gave him the number of his days and time, and gave him power over all things that are upon the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He put the fear of him upon all flesh, and he had dominion over beasts and fowls.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He created of him a helpmate like to himself, he gave them counsel, and a tongue, and eyes, and ears, and a heart to devise: and he filled them with the knowledge of understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He created in them the science of the spirit, he filled their heart with wisdom, and shewed them both good and evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He set his eye upon their hearts to shew them the greatness of his works:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That they might praise the name which he hath sanctified: and glory in his wondrous acts that they might declare the glorious things of his works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover he gave them instructions, and the law of life for an inheritance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He made an everlasting covenant with them, and he shewed them his justice and judgments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And their eye saw the majesty of his glory, and their ears heard his glorious voice, and he said to them: Beware of all iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he gave to every one of them commandment concerning his neighbour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Their ways are always before him, they are not hidden from his eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Over every nation he set a ruler.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Israel was made the manifest portion of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all their works are as the sun in the sight of God: and his eyes are continually upon their ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Their covenants were not hid by their iniquity, and all their iniquities are in the sight of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The alms of a man is as a signet with him, and shall preserve the grace of a man as the apple of the eye:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And afterward he shall rise up, and shall render them their reward, to every one upon their own head, and shall turn them down into the bowels of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But to the penitent he hath given the way of justice, and he hath strengthened them that were fainting in patience, and hath appointed to them the lot of truth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Turn to the Lord, and forsake thy sins:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Make thy prayer before the face of the Lord, and offend less.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Return to the Lord, and turn away from thy injustice, and greatly hate abomination.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And know the justices and judgments of God, and stand firm in the lot set before thee, and in prayer to the most high God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go to the side of the holy age, with them that live and give praise to God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Tarry not in the error of the ungodly, give glory before death. Praise perisheth from the dead as nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give thanks whilst thou art living, whilst thou art alive and in health thou shalt give thanks, and shalt praise God, and shalt glory in his mercies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How great is the mercy of the Lord, and his forgiveness to them that turn to him!

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For all things cannot be in men, because the son of man is not immortal, and they are delighted with the vanity of evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What is brighter than the sun; yet it shall be eclipsed. Or what is more wicked than that which flesh and blood hath invented? and this shall be reproved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He beholdeth the power of the height of heaven: and all men are earth and ashes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that liveth for ever created all things together. God only shall be justified, and he remaineth an invincible king for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is able to declare his works?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For who shall search out his glorious acts?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And who shall show forth the power of his majesty? or who shall be able to declare his mercy?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nothing may be taken away, nor added, neither is it possible to find out the glorious works of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When a man hath done, then shall he begin: and when he leaveth off, he shall be at a loss.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What is man, and what is his grace? and what is his good, or what is his evil?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The number of the days of men at the most are a hundred years, as a drop of water of the sea are they esteemed: and as a pebble of the sand, so are a few years compared to eternity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore God is patient in them, and poureth forth his mercy upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath seen the presumption of their heart that it is wicked, and hath known their end that it is evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore hath he filled up his mercy in their favour, and hath shewn them the way of justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The compassion of man is toward his neighbour: but the mercy of God is upon all flesh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath mercy, and teacheth, and correcteth, as a shepherd doth his flock.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath mercy on him that receiveth the discipline of mercy, and that maketh haste in his judgments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, in thy good deeds, make no complaint, and when thou givest any thing, add not grief by an evil word.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall not the dew assuage the heat? so also the good word is better than the gift.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lo, is not a word better than a gift? but both are with a justified man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool will upbraid bitterly: and a gift of one ill taught consumeth the eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before judgment prepare thee justice, and learn before thou speak.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before sickness take a medicine, and before judgment examine thyself, and thou shalt find mercy in the sight of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Humble thyself before thou art sick, and in the time of sickness shew thy conversation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let nothing hinder thee from praying always, and be not afraid to be justified even to death: for the reward of God continueth for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before prayer prepare thy soul: and be not as a man that tempteth God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember the wrath that shall be at the last day, and the time of repaying when he shall turn away his face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember poverty in the time of abundance, and the necessities of poverty in the day of riches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From the morning until the evening the time shall be changed, and all these are swift in the eyes of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise man will fear in every thing, and in the days of sins will beware of sloth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Every man of understanding knoweth wisdom, and will give praise to him that findeth her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that were of good understanding in words, have also done wisely themselves: and have understood truth and justice, and have poured forth proverbs and judgments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go not after thy lusts, but turn away from thy own will.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou give to thy soul her desires, she will make thee a joy to thy enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take no pleasure in riotous assemblies, be they ever so small: for their concertation is continual.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Make not thyself poor by borrowing to contribute to feasts when thou hast nothing in thy purse: for thou shalt be an enemy to thy own life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A workman that is a drunkard shall not be rich: and he that contemneth small things, shall fall by little and little.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wine and women make wise men fall off, and shall rebuke the prudent:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he that joineth himself to harlots, will be wicked. Rottenness and worms shall inherit him, and he shall be lifted up for a greater example, and his soul shall be taken away out of the number.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is hasty to give credit, is light of heart, and shall be lessened: and he that sinneth against his own soul, shall be despised.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that rejoiceth in iniquity, shall be censured, and he that hateth chastisement, shall have less life: and he that hateth babbling, extinguisheth evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that sinneth against his own soul, shall repent: and he that is delighted with wickedness, shall be condemned.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Rehearse not again a wicked and harsh word, and thou shalt not fare the worse.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Tell not thy mind to friend or foe: and if there be a sin with thee, disclose it not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he will hearken to thee, and will watch thee, and as it were defending thy sin he will hate thee, and so will he be with thee always.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hast thou heard a word against thy neighbour? let it die within thee, trusting that it will not burst thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At the hearing of a word the fool is in travail, as a woman groaning in the bringing forth a child.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As an arrow that sticketh in a man’s thigh: so is a word in the heart of a fool.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Reprove a friend, lest he may not have understood, and say: I did it not: or if he did it, that he may do it no more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Reprove thy neighbour, for it may be he hath not said it: and if he hath said it, that he may not say it again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Admonish thy friend: for there is often a fault committed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And believe not every word. There is one, that slippeth with the tongue, but not from his heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For who is there that hath not offended with his tongue? Admonish thy neighbour before thou threaten him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And give place to the fear of the most High: for the fear of God is all wisdom, and therein is to fear God, and the disposition of the law is in all wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the learning of wickedness is not wisdom: and the device of sinners is not prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a subtle wickedness, and the same is detestable: and there is a man that is foolish, wanting in wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is a man that hath less wisdom, and wanteth understanding, with the fear of God, than he that aboundeth in understanding, and transgresseth the law of the most High.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is an exquisite subtilty, and the same is unjust.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there is one that uttereth an exact word telling the truth. There is one that humbleth himself wickedly, and his interior is full of deceit:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there is one that submitteth himself exceedingly with a great lowliness: and there is one that casteth down his countenance, and maketh as if he did not see that which is unknown:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if he be hindered from sinning for want of power, if he shall find opportunity to do evil, he will do it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man is known by his look, and a wise man, when thou meetest him, is known by his countenance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The attire of the body, and the laughter of the teeth, and the gait of the man, shew what he is.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a lying rebuke in the anger of an injurious man: and there is a judgment that is not allowed to be good: and there is one that holdeth his peace, he is wise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How much better is it to reprove, than to be angry, and not to hinder him that confesseth in prayer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lust of an eunuch shall deflour a young maiden:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So is he that by violence executeth of the unwise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How good is it, when thou art reproved, to shew repentance! for so thou shalt escape wilful sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is one that holdeth his peace, that is found wise: and there is another that is hateful, that is bold in speech.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is one that holdeth his peace, because he knoweth not what to say: and there is another that holdeth his peace, knowing the proper time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise man will hold his peace till he see opportunity: but a babbler, and a fool, will regard no time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that useth many words shall hurt his own soul: and he that taketh authority to himself unjustly shall be hated.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is success in evil things to a man without discipline, and there is a finding that turneth to loss.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a gift that is not profitable: and there is a gift, the recompense of which is double.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is an abasement because of glory: and there is one that shall lift up his head from a low estate.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is that buyeth much for a small price, and restoreth the same sevenfold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man wise in words shall make himself beloved: but the graces of fools shall be poured out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The gift of the fool shall do thee no good: for his eyes are sevenfold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He will give a few things, and upbraid much: and the opening of his mouth is the kindling of a fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To day a man lendeth, and to morrow he asketh it again: such a man as this is hateful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool shall have no friend, and there shall be no thanks for his good deeds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they that eat his bread, are of a false tongue. How often, and how many will laugh him to scorn!

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he doth not distribute with right understanding that which was to be had: in like manner also that which was not to be had.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The slipping of a false tongue is as one that falleth on the pavement: so the fall of the wicked shall come speedily.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man without grace is as a vain fable, it shall be continually in the mouth of the unwise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A parable coming out of a fool’s mouth shall be rejected: for he doth not speak it in due season.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is that is hindered from sinning through want, and in his rest he shall be pricked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is that will destroy his own soul through shamefacedness, and by occasion of an unwise person he will destroy it: and by respect of person he will destroy himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is that for bashfulness promiseth to his friend, and maketh him his enemy for nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A lie is a foul blot in a man, and yet it will be continually in the mouth of men without discipline.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A thief is better than a man that is always lying: but both of them shall inherit destruction.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The manners of lying men are without honour: and their confusion is with them without ceasing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise man shall advance himself with his words, and a prudent man shall please the great ones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that tilleth his land shall make a high heap of corn: and he that worketh justice shall be exalted: and he that pleaseth great men shall escape iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Presents and gifts blind the eyes of judges, and make them dumb in the mouth, so that they cannot correct.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O Wisdom that is hid, and treasure that is not seen: what profit is there in them both?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is he that hideth his folly, than the man that hideth his wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, hast thou sinned? do so no more: but for thy former sins also pray that they may be forgiven thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Flee from sins as from the face of a serpent: for if thou comest near them, they will take hold of thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The teeth thereof are the teeth of a lion, killing the souls of men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All iniquity is like a two-edged sword, there is no remedy for the wound thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Injuries and wrongs will waste riches: and the house that is very rich shall be brought to nothing by pride: so the substance of the proud shall be rooted out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The prayer out of the mouth of the poor shall reach the ears of God, and judgment shall come for him speedily.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hateth to be reproved walketh in the trace of a sinner: and he that feareth God will turn to his own heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is mighty by a bold tongue is known afar off, but a wise man knoweth to slip by him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that buildeth his house at other men’s charges, is as he that gathereth himself stones to build in the winter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The congregation of sinners is like tow heaped together, and the end of them is a flame of fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The way of sinners is made plain with stones, and in their end is hell, and darkness, and pains.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that keepeth justice shall get the understanding thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The perfection of the fear of God is wisdom and understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is not wise in good, will not be taught.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But there is a wisdom that aboundeth in evil: and there is no understanding where there is bitterness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The knowledge of a wise man shall abound like a flood, and his counsel continueth like a fountain of life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of a fool is like a broken vessel, and no wisdom at all shall it hold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man of sense will praise every wise word he shall hear, and will apply it to himself: the luxurious man hath heard it, and it shall displease him, and he will cast it behind his back.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The talking of a fool is like a burden in the way: but in the lips of the wise, grace shall be found.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mouth of the prudent is sought after in the church, and they will think upon his words in their hearts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a house that is destroyed, so is wisdom to a fool: and the knowledge of the unwise is as words without sense.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Doctrine to a fool is as fetters on the feet, and like manacles on the right hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool lifteth up his voice in laughter: but a wise man will scarce laugh low to himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Learning to the prudent is as an ornament of gold, and like a bracelet upon his right arm.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The foot of a fool is soon in his neighbour’s house: but a man of experience will be abashed at the person of the mighty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool will peep through the window into the house: but he that is well taught will stand without.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is the folly of a man to hearken at the door: and a wise man will be grieved with the disgrace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lips of the unwise will be telling foolish things: but the words of the wise shall be weighed in a balance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of fools is in their mouth: and the mouth of wise men is in their heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: While the ungodly curseth the devil, he curseth his own soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The talebearer shall defile his own soul, and shall be hated by all: and he that shall abide with him shall be hateful: the silent and wise man shall be honoured.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sluggard is pelted with a dirty stone, and all men will speak of his disgrace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sluggard is pelted with the dung of oxen: and every one that toucheth him will shake his hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A son ill taught is the confusion of the father: and a foolish daughter shall be to his loss.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise daughter shall bring an inheritance to her husband: but she that confoundeth, becometh a disgrace to her father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She that is bold shameth both her father and husband, and will not be inferior to the ungodly: and shall be disgraced by them both.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A tale out of time is like music in mourning: but the stripes and instruction of wisdom are never out of time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that teacheth a fool, is like one that glueth a potsherd together.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that telleth a word to him that heareth not, is like one that waketh a man out of a deep sleep.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He speaketh with one that is asleep, who uttereth wisdom to a fool: and in the end of the discourse he saith: Who is this?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Weep for the dead, for his light hath failed: and weep for the fool, for his understanding faileth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Weep but a little for the dead, for he is at rest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the wicked life of a wicked fool is worse than death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mourning for the dead is seven days: but for a fool and an ungodly man all the days of their life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Talk not much with a fool and go not with him that hath no sense.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Keep thyself from him, that thou mayst not have trouble, and thou shalt not be defiled with his sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Turn away from him, and thou shalt find rest, and shalt not be wearied out with his folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What is heavier than lead? and what other name hath he but fool?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sand and salt, and a mass of iron is easier to bear, than a man without sense, that is both foolish and wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A frame of wood bound together in the foundation of a building, shall not be loosed: so neither shall the heart that is established by advised counsel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The thought of him that is wise at all times, shall not be depraved by fear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As pales set in high places, and plasterings made without cost, will not stand against the face of the wind:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So also a fearful heart in the imagination of a fool shall not resist against the violence of fear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a fearful heart in the thought of a fool at all times will not fear, so neither shall he that continueth always in the commandments of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that pricketh the eye, bringeth out tears: and he that pricketh the heart, bringeth forth resentment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that flingeth a stone at birds, shall drive them away: so he that upbraideth his friend, breaketh friendship.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Although thou hast drawn a sword at a friend, despair not: for there may be a returning. To a friend,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou hast opened a sad mouth, fear not, for there may be a reconciliation: except upbraiding, and reproach, and pride, and disclosing of secrets, or a treacherous wound: for in all these cases a friend will flee away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Keep fidelity with a friend in his poverty, that in his prosperity also thou mayst rejoice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the time of his trouble continue faithful to him, that thou mayst also be heir with him in his inheritance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the vapour of a chimney, and the smoke of the fire goeth up before the fire: so also injurious words, and reproaches, and threats, before blood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will not be ashamed to salute a friend, neither will I hide myself from his face: and if any evil happen to me by him, I will bear it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But every one that shall hear it, will beware of him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who will set a guard before my mouth, and a sure seal upon my lips, that I fall not by them, and that my tongue destroy me not?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O Lord, father, and sovereign ruler of my life, leave me not to their counsel: nor suffer me to fall by them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who will set scourges over my thoughts, and the discipline of wisdom over my heart, that they spare me not in their ignorances, and that their sins may not appear:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lest my ignorances increase, and my offences be multiplied, and my sins abound, and I fall before my adversaries, and my enemy rejoice over me?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O Lord, father, and God of my life, leave me not to their devices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give me not haughtiness of my eyes, and turn away from me all coveting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take from me the greediness of the belly, and let not the lusts of the flesh take hold of me, and give me not over to a shameless and foolish mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear, O ye children, the discipline of the mouth, and he that will keep it shall not perish by his lips, nor be brought to fall into most wicked works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A sinner is caught in his own vanity, and the proud and the evil speakers shall fall thereby.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let not thy mouth be accustomed to swearing: for in it there are many falls.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let not the naming of God be usual in thy mouth, and meddle not with the names of saints, for thou shalt not escape free from them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as a slave daily put to the question, is never without a blue mark: so every one that sweareth, and nameth, shall not be wholly pure from sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man that sweareth much, shall be filled with iniquity, and a scourge shall not depart from his house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if he make it void, his sin shall be upon him, and if he dissemble it, he offendeth double:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if he swear in vain, he shall not be justified: for his house shall be filled with his punishment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is also another speech opposite to death, let it not be found in the inheritance of Jacob.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For from the merciful all these things shall be taken away, and they shall not wallow in sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let not thy mouth be accustomed to indiscreet speech: for therein is the word of sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember thy father and thy mother, for thou sittest in the midst of great men:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lest God forget thee in their sight, and thou, by thy daily custom be infatuated and suffer reproach: and wish that thou hadst not been born, and curse the day of thy nativity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The man that is accustomed to opprobrious words, will never be corrected all the days of his life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Two sorts of men multiply sins, and the third bringeth wrath and destruction.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A hot soul is a burning fire, it will never be quenched, till it devour some thing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a man that is wicked in the mouth of his flesh, will not leave off till he hath kindled a fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To a man that is a fornicator all bread is sweet, he will not be weary of sinning unto the end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Every man that passeth beyond his own bed, despising his own soul, and saying: Who seeth me?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Darkness compasseth me about, and the walls cover me, and no man seeth me: whom do I fear? the most High will not remember my sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he understandeth not that his eye seeth all things, for such a man’s fear driveth him from the fear of God, and the eyes of men fearing him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he knoweth not that the eyes of the Lord are far brighter than the sun, beholding round about all the ways of men, and the bottom of the deep, and looking into the hearts of men, into the most hidden parts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For all things were known to the Lord God, before they were created: so also after they were perfected he beholdeth all things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This man shall be punished in the streets of the city, and he shall be chased as a colt: and where he suspected not, he shall be taken.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he shall be in disgrace with all men, because he understood not the fear of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So every woman also that leaveth her husband, and bringeth in an heir by another:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For first she hath been unfaithful to the law of the most High: and secondly, she hath offended against her husband: thirdly, she hath fornicated in adultery, and hath gotten her children of another man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This woman shall be brought into the assembly, and inquisition shall be made of her children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Her children shall not take root, and her branches shall bring forth no fruit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She shall leave her memory to be cursed, and her infamy shall not be blotted out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that remain shall know, that there is nothing better than the fear of God: and that there is nothing sweeter than to have regard to the commandments of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is great glory to follow the Lord: for length of days shall be received from him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom shall praise her own self, and shall be honoured in God, and shall glory in the midst of her people,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And shall open her mouth in the churches of the most High, and shall glorify herself in the sight of his power,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in the midst of her own people she shall be exalted, and shall be admired in the holy assembly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in the multitude of the elect she shall have praise, and among the blessed she shall be blessed, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I came out of the mouth of the most High, the firstborn before all creatures:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I made that in the heavens there should rise light that never faileth, and as a cloud I covered all the earth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I dwelt in the highest places, and my throne is in a pillar of a cloud.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I alone have compassed the circuit of heaven, and have penetrated into the bottom of the deep, and have walked in the waves of the sea,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And have stood in all the earth: and in every people,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in every nation I have had the chief rule:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And by my power I have trodden under my feet the hearts of all the high and low: and in all these I sought rest, and I shall abide in the inheritance of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the creator of all things commanded, and said to me: and he that made me, rested in my tabernacle,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to me: Let thy dwelling be in Jacob, and thy inheritance in Israel, and take root in my elect.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From the beginning, and before the world, was I created, and unto the world to come I shall not cease to be, and in the holy dwelling place I have ministered before him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And so was I established in Sion, and in the holy city likewise I rested, and my power was in Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I took root in an honourable people, and in the portion of my God his inheritance, and my abode is in the full assembly of saints.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I was exalted like a cedar in Libanus, and as a cypress tree on mount Sion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I was exalted like a palm tree in Cades, and as a rose plant in Jericho:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a fair olive tree in the plains, and as a plane tree by the water in the streets, was I exalted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I gave a sweet smell like cinnamon, and aromatical balm: I yielded a sweet odour like the best myrrh:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I perfumed my dwelling as storax, and galbanum, and onyx, and aloes, and as the frankincense not cut, and my odour is as the purest balm.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have stretched out my branches as the turpentine tree, and my branches are of honour and grace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the vine I have brought forth a pleasant odour: and my flowers are the fruit of honour and riches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I am the mother of fair love, and of fear, and of knowledge, and of holy hope.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In me is all grace of the way and of the truth, in me is all hope of life and of virtue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come over to me, all ye that desire me, and be filled with my fruits.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For my spirit is sweet above honey, and my inheritance above honey and the honeycomb.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My memory is unto everlasting generations.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that eat me, shall yet hunger: and they that drink me, shall yet thirst.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hearkeneth to me, shall not be confounded: and they that work by me, shall not sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that explain me shall have life everlasting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All these things are the book of life, and the covenant of the most High, and the knowledge of truth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moses commanded a law in the precepts of justices, and an inheritance to the house of Jacob, and the promises to Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He appointed to David his servant to raise up of him a most mighty king, and sitting on the throne of glory for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who filleth up wisdom as the Phison, and as the Tigris in the days of the new fruits.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who maketh understanding to abound as the Euphrates, who multiplieth it as the Jordan in the time of harvest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who sendeth knowledge as the light, and riseth up as Gehon in the time of the vintage.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who first hath perfect knowledge of her, and a weaker shall not search her out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For her thoughts are more vast than the sea, and her counsels more deep than the great ocean.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I, wisdom, have poured out rivers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I, like a brook out of a river of a mighty water; I, like a channel of a river, and like an aqueduct, came out of paradise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I said: I will water my garden of plants, and I will water abundantly the fruits of my meadow.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold my brook became a great river, and my river came near to a sea:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I make doctrine to shine forth to all as the morning light, and I will declare it afar off.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will penetrate to all the lower parts of the earth, and will behold all that sleep, and will enlighten all that hope in the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will yet pour out doctrine as prophecy, and will leave it to them that seek wisdom, and will not cease to instruct their offspring even to the holy age.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: See ye that I have not laboured myself only, but for all that seek out the truth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With three things my spirit is pleased, which are approved before God and men:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The concord of brethren, and the love of neighbours, and man and wife that agree well together.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Three sorts my soul hateth, and I am greatly grieved at their life:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A poor man that is proud: a rich man that is a liar: an old man that is a fool, and doting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The things that thou hast not gathered in thy youth, how shalt thou find them in thy old age?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O how comely is judgment for a grey head, and for ancients to know counsel!

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O how comely is wisdom for the aged, and understanding and counsel to men of honour!

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Much experience is the crown of old men, and the fear of God is their glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nine things that are not to be imagined by the heart have I magnified, and the tenth I will utter to men with my tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man that hath joy of his children: and he that liveth and seeth the fall of his enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is he that dwelleth with a wise woman, and that hath not slipped with his tongue, and that hath not served such as are unworthy of him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is he that findeth a true friend, and that declareth justice to an ear that heareth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How great is he that findeth wisdom and knowledge! but there is none above him that feareth the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of God hath set itself above all things:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is the man, to whom it is given to have the fear of God: he that holdeth it, to whom shall he be likened?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fear of God is the beginning of his love: and the beginning of faith is to be fast joined unto it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sadness of the heart is every plague: and the wickedness of a woman is all evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a man will choose any plague, but the plague of the heart:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And any wickedness, but the wickedness of a woman:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And any affliction, but the affliction from them that hate him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And any revenge, but the revenge of enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is no head worse than the head of a serpent:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there is no anger above the anger of a woman. It will be more agreeable to abide with a lion and a dragon, than to dwell with a wicked woman.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wickedness of a woman changeth her face: and she darkeneth her countenance as a bear: and sheweth it like sackcloth. In the midst of her neighbours,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Her husband groaned, and hearing he sighed a little.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All malice is short to the malice of a woman, let the lot of sinners fall upon her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the climbing of a sandy way is to the feet of the aged, so is a wife full of tongue to a quiet man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Look not upon a woman’s beauty, and desire not a woman for beauty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A woman’s anger, and impudence, and confusion is great.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A woman, if she have superiority, is contrary to her husband.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wicked woman abateth the courage, and maketh a heavy countenance, and a wounded heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Feeble hands, and disjointed knees, a woman that doth not make her husband happy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From the woman came the beginning of sin, and by her we all die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give no issue to thy water, no, not a little: nor to a wicked woman liberty to gad abroad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If she walk not at thy hand, she will confound thee in the sight of thy enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Cut her off from thy flesh, lest she always abuse thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Happy is the husband of a good wife: for the number of his years is double.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A virtuous woman rejoiceth her husband, and shall fulfil the years of his life in peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A good wife is a good portion, she shall be given in the portion of them that fear God, to a man for his good deeds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Rich or poor, if his heart is good, his countenance shall be cheerful at all times.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Of three things my heart hath been afraid, and at the fourth my face hath trembled:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The accusation of a city, and the gathering together of the people:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a false calumny, all are more grievous than death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A jealous woman is the grief and mourning of the heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With a jealous woman is a scourge of the tongue which communicateth with all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a yoke of oxen that is moved to and fro, so also is a wicked woman: he that hath hold of her, is as he that taketh hold of a scorpion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A drunken woman is a great wrath: and her reproach and shame shall not be hid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fornication of a woman shall be known by the haughtiness of her eyes and by her eyelids.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: On a daughter that turneth not away herself, set a strict watch: lest finding an opportunity she abuse herself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take heed of the impudence of her eyes, and wonder not if she slight thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She will open her mouth as a thirsty traveller to the fountain, and will drink of every water near her, and will sit down by every hedge, and open her quiver against every arrow, until she fail.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The grace of a diligent woman shall delight her husband, and shall fat his bones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Her discipline is the gift of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Such is a wise and silent woman, and there is nothing so much worth as a well instructed soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A holy and shamefaced woman is grace upon grace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And no price is worthy of a continent soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the sun when it riseth to the world in the high places of God, so is the beauty of a good wife for the ornament of her house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the lamp shining upon the holy candlestick, so is the beauty of the face in a ripe age,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As golden pillars upon bases of silver, so are the firm feet upon the soles of a steady woman.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As everlasting foundations upon a solid rock, so the commandments of God in the heart of a holy woman.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At two things my heart is grieved, and the third bringeth anger upon me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man of war fainting through poverty, and a man of sense despised:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he that passeth over from justice to sin, God hath prepared such an one for the sword.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Two sorts of callings have appeared to me hard and dangerous: a merchant is hardly free from negligence: and a huckster shall not be justified from the sins of the lips.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Through poverty many have sinned: and he that seeketh to be enriched, turneth away his eye.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a stake sticketh fast in the midst of the joining of stones, so also in the midst of selling and buying, sin shall stick fast.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sin shall be destroyed with the sinner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
