text: Unless thou hold thyself diligently in the fear of the Lord, thy house shall quickly be overthrown.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As when one sifteth with a sieve, the dust will remain: so will the perplexity of a man in his thoughts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The furnace trieth the potter’s vessels, and the trial of affliction just men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the dressing of a tree sheweth the fruit thereof, so a word out of the thought of the heart of man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Praise not a man before he speaketh, for this is the trial of men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou followest justice, thou shalt obtain her: and shalt put her on as a long robe of honour, and thou shalt dwell with her: and she shall protect thee for ever, and in the day of acknowledgment thou shalt find a strong foundation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Birds resort unto their like: so truth will return to them that practise her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lion always lieth in wait for prey: so do sins for them that work iniquities.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A holy man continueth in wisdom as the sun: but a fool is changed as the moon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the midst of the unwise keep in the word till its time: but be continually among men that think.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The discourse of sinners is hateful, and their laughter is at the pleasures of sin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The speech that sweareth much shall make the hair of the head stand upright: and its irreverence shall make one stop his ears.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the quarrels of the proud is the shedding of blood: and their cursing is a grievous hearing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that discloseth the secret of a friend loseth his credit, and shall never find a friend to his mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Love thy neighbour, and be joined to him with fidelity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if thou discover his secrets, follow no more after him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as a man that destroyeth his friend, so is he that destroyeth the friendship of his neighbour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as one that letteth a bird go out of his hand, so hast thou let thy neighbour go, and thou shalt not get him again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Follow after him no more, for he is gone afar off, he is fled, as a roe escaped out of the snare because his soul is wounded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou canst no more bind him up. And of a curse there is reconciliation:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But to disclose the secrets of a friend, leaveth no hope to an unhappy soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that winketh with the eye forgeth wicked things, and no man will cast him off:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the sight of thy eyes he will sweeten his mouth, and will admire thy words: but at the last he will writhe his mouth, and on thy words he will lay a stumblingblock.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have hated many things but not like him, and the Lord will hate him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If one cast a stone on high, it will fall upon his own head: and the deceitful stroke will wound the deceitful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that diggeth a pit, shall fall into it: and he that setteth a stone for his neighbour, shall stumble upon it: and he that layeth a snare for another, shall perish in it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A mischievous counsel shall be rolled back upon the author, and he shall not know from whence it cometh to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Mockery and reproach are of the proud, and vengeance as a lion shall lie in wait for him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They shall perish in a snare that are delighted with the fall of the just: and sorrow shall consume them before they die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Anger and fury are both of them abominable, and the sinful man shall be subject to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that seeketh to revenge himself, shall find vengeance from the Lord, and he will surely keep his sins in remembrance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forgive thy neighbour if he hath hurt thee: and then shall thy sins be forgiven to thee when thou prayest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Man to man reserveth anger, and doth he seek remedy of God?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath no mercy on a man like himself, and doth he entreat for his own sins?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that is but flesh, nourisheth anger, and doth he ask forgiveness of God? who shall obtain pardon for his sins?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember thy last things, and let enmity cease:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For corruption and death hang over in his commandments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember the fear of God, and be not angry with thy neighbour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember the covenant of the most High, and overlook the ignorance of thy neighbour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Refrain from strife, and thou shalt diminish thy sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For a passionate man kindleth strife, and a sinful man will trouble his friends, and bring in debate in the midst of them that are at peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as the wood of the forest is, so the fire burneth, and as a man’s strength is, so shall his anger be, and according to his riches he shall increase his anger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A hasty contention kindleth a fire and a hasty quarrel sheddeth blood and a tongue that beareth witness bringeth death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou blow the spark, it shall burn as a fire: and if thou spit upon it, it shall be quenched: both come out of the mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The whisperer and the double tongue is accursed: for he hath troubled many that were at peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The tongue of a third person hath disquieted many, and scattered them from nation to nation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It hath destroyed the strong cities of the rich, and hath overthrown the houses of great men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It hath cut in pieces the forces of people, and undone strong nations.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The tongue of a third person hath cast out valiant women, and deprived them of their labours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hearkeneth to it, shall never have rest, neither shall he have a friend in whom he may repose.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The stroke of a whip maketh a blue mark: but the stroke of the tongue will break the bones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many have fallen by the edge of the sword, but not so many as have perished by their own tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is he that is defended from a wicked tongue, that hath not passed into the wrath thereof, and that hath not drawn the yoke thereof, and hath not been bound in its bands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For its yoke is a yoke of iron: and its bands are bands of brass.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The death thereof is a most evil death: and hell is preferable to it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Its continuance shall not be for a long time, but it shall possess the ways of the unjust: and the just shall not be burnt with its flame.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that forsake God shall fall into it, and it shall burn in them, and shall not be quenched, and it shall be sent upon them as a lion, and as a leopard it shall tear them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hedge in thy ears with thorns, hear not a wicked tongue, and make doors and bars to thy mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Melt down thy gold and silver, and make a balance for thy words, and a just bridle for thy mouth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And take heed lest thou slip with thy tongue, and fall in the sight of thy enemies who lie in wait for thee, and thy fall be incurable unto death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that sheweth mercy, lendeth to his neighbour: and he that is stronger in hand, keepeth the commandments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lend to thy neighbour in the time of his need, and pay thou thy neighbour again in due time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Keep thy word, and deal faithfully with him: and thou shalt always find that which is necessary for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many have looked upon a thing lent as a thing found, and have given trouble to them that helped them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Till they receive, they kiss the hands of the lender, and in promises they humble their voice:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when they should repay, they will ask time, and will return tedious and murmuring words, and will complain of the time:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if he be able to pay, he will stand off, he will scarce pay one half, and will count it as if he had found it:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if not, he will defraud him of his money, and he shall get him for an enemy without cause.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he will pay him with reproaches and curses, and instead of honour and good turn will repay him injuries.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many have refused to lend, not out of wickedness, but they were afraid to be defrauded without cause.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But yet towards the poor be thou more hearty, and delay not to shew him mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Help the poor because of the commandment: and send him not away empty handed because of his poverty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lose thy money for thy brother and thy friend: and hide it not under a stone to be lost.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Place thy treasure in the commandments of the most High, and it shall bring thee more profit than gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shut up alms in the heart of the poor, and it shall obtain help for thee against all evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better than the shield of the mighty, and better than the spear:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It shall fight for thee against thy enemy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A good man is surety for his neighbour: and he that hath lost shame, will leave him to himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forget not the kindness of thy surety: for he hath given his life for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sinner and the unclean fleeth from his surety.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A sinner attributeth to himself the goods of his surety: and he that is of an unthankful mind will leave him that delivered him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man is surety for his neighbour: and when he hath lost all shame, he shall forsake him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Evil suretyship hath undone many of good estate, and hath tossed them as a wave of the sea.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It hath made powerful men to go from place to place round about, and they have wandered in strange countries.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A sinner that transgresseth the commandment of the Lord, shall fall into an evil suretyship: and he that undertaketh many things, shall fall into judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Recover thy neighbour according to thy power, and take heed to thyself that thou fall not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The chief thing for man’s life is water and bread, and clothing, and a house to cover shame.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is the poor man’s fare under a roof of boards, than sumptuous cheer abroad in another man’s house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be contented with little instead of much, and thou shalt not hear the reproach of going abroad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is a miserable life to go as a guest from house to house: for where a man is a stranger, he shall not deal confidently, nor open his mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall entertain and feed, and give drink to the unthankful, and moreover he shall hear bitter words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go, stranger, and furnish the table, and give others to eat what thou hast in thy hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give place to the honourable presence of my friends: for I want my house, my brother being to be lodged with me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These things are grievous to a man of understanding: the upbraiding of houseroom, and the reproaching of the lender.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that loveth his son, frequently chastiseth him, that he may rejoice in his latter end, and not grope after the doors of his neighbours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that instructeth his son shall be praised in him, and shall glory in him in the midst of them of his household.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that teacheth his son, maketh his enemy jealous, and in the midst of his friends he shall glory in him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His father is dead, and he is as if he were not dead: for he hath left one behind him that is like himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: While he lived he saw and rejoiced in him: and when he died he was not sorrowful, neither was he confounded before his enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he left behind him a defender of his house against his enemies, and one that will requite kindness to his friends.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the souls of his sons he shall bind up his wounds, and at every cry his bowels shall be troubled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A horse not broken becometh stubborn, and a child left to himself will become headstrong.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give thy son his way, and he shall make thee afraid: play with him, and he shall make thee sorrowful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Laugh not with him, lest thou have sorrow, and at the last thy teeth be set on edge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give him not liberty in his youth, and wink not at his devices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Bow down his neck while he is young, and beat his sides while he is a child, lest he grow stubborn, and regard thee not, and so be a sorrow of heart to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Instruct thy son, and labour about him, lest his lewd behaviour be an offence to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is a poor man who is sound, and strong of constitution, than a rich man who is weak and afflicted with evils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Health of the soul in holiness of justice, is better than all gold and silver: and a sound body, than immense revenues.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is no riches above the riches of the health of the body: and there is no pleasure above the joy of the heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is death than a bitter life, and everlasting rest, than continual sickness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Good things that are hidden in a mouth that is shut, are as messes of meat set about a grave.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What good shall an offering do to an idol? for it can neither eat, nor smell:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So is he that is persecuted by the Lord, bearing the reward of his iniquity:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He seeth with his eyes, and groaneth, as an eunuch embracing a virgin, and sighing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give not up thy soul to sadness, and afflict not thyself in thy own counsel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The joyfulness of the heart, is the life of a man, and a never failing treasure of holiness: and the joy of a man is length of life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Have pity on thy own soul, pleasing God, and contain thyself: gather up thy heart in his holiness: and drive away sadness far from thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For sadness hath killed many, and there is no profit in it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Envy and anger shorten a man’s days, and pensiveness will bring old age before the time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A cheerful and good heart is always feasting: for his banquets are prepared with diligence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Watching for riches consumeth the flesh, and the thought thereof driveth away sleep.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The thinking beforehand turneth away the understanding, and a grievous sickness maketh the soul sober.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The rich man hath laboured in gathering riches together, and when he resteth he shall be filled with his goods.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The poor man hath laboured in his low way of life, and in the end he is still poor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that loveth gold, shall not be justified: and he that followeth after corruption, shall be filled with it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many have been brought to fall for gold, and the beauty thereof hath been their ruin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Gold is a stumblingblock to them that sacrifice to it: woe to them that eagerly follow after it, and every fool shall perish by it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is the rich man that is found without blemish: and that hath not gone after gold, nor put his trust in money nor in treasures.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is he, and we will praise him? for he hath done wonderful things in his life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who hath been tried thereby, and made perfect, he shall have glory everlasting. He that could have transgressed, and hath not transgressed: and could do evil things, and hath not done them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore are his goods established in the Lord, and all the church of the saints shall declare his alms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Art thou set at a great table? be not the first to open thy mouth upon it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: There are many things which are upon it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember that a wicked eye is evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What is created more wicked than an eye? therefore shall it weep over all the face when it shall see.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Stretch not out thy hand first, lest being disgraced with envy thou be put to confusion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not hasty in a feast.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Judge of the disposition of thy neighbour by thyself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Use as a frugal man the things that are set before thee: lest if thou eatest much, thou be hated.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Leave off first, for manners’ sake: and exceed not, lest thou offend.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if thou sittest among many, reach not thy hand out first of all, and be not the first to ask for drink.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How sufficient is a little wine for a man well taught, and in sleeping thou shalt not be uneasy with it, and thou shalt feel no pain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Watching, and choler, and gripes, are with an intemperate man:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sound and wholesome sleep with a moderate man: he shall sleep till morning, and his soul shall be delighted with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if thou hast been forced to eat much, arise, go out, and vomit: and it shall refresh thee, and thou shalt not bring sickness upon thy body.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear me, my son, and despise me not: and in the end thou shalt find my words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In all thy works be quick, and no infirmity shall come to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lips of many shall bless him that is liberal of his bread, and the testimony of his truth is faithful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Against him that is niggardly of his bread, the city will murmur, and the testimony of his niggardliness is true.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Challenge not them that love wine: for wine hath destroyed very many.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Fire trieth hard iron: so wine drunk to excess shall rebuke the hearts of the proud.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wine taken with sobriety is equal life to men: if thou drink it moderately, thou shalt be sober.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What is his life, who is diminished with wine?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What taketh away life? death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wine was created from the beginning to make men joyful, and not to make them drunk.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wine drunken with moderation is the joy of the soul and the heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sober drinking is health to soul and body.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wine drunken with excess raiseth quarrels, and wrath, and many ruins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wine drunken with excess is bitterness of the soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heat of drunkenness is the stumblingblock of the fool, lessening strength and causing wounds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Rebuke not thy neighbour in a banquet of wine: and despise him not in his mirth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Speak not to him words of reproach: and press him not in demanding again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Have they made thee ruler? be not lifted up: be among them as one of them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Have care of them, and so sit down, and when thou hast acquitted thyself of all thy charge, take thy place:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That thou mayst rejoice for them, and receive a crown as an ornament of grace, and get the honour of the contribution.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Speak, thou that art elder: for it becometh thee,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To speak the first word with careful knowledge, and hinder not music.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where there is no hearing, pour not out words, and be not lifted up out of season with thy wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A concert of music in a banquet of wine is as a carbuncle set in gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As a signet of an emerald in a work of gold: so is the melody of music with pleasant and moderate wine.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear in silence, and for thy reverence good grace shall come to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Young man, scarcely speak in thy own cause.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou be asked twice, let thy answer be short.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In many things be as if thou wert ignorant, and hear in silence and withal seeking.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the company of great men take not upon thee: and when the ancients are present, speak not much.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before a storm goeth lightning: and before shamefacedness goeth favour: and for thy reverence good grace shall come to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And at the time of rising be not slack: but be first to run home to thy house, and there withdraw thyself, and there take thy pastime.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And do what thou hast a mind, but not in sin or proud speech.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And for all these things bless the Lord, that made thee, and that replenisheth thee with all his good things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that feareth the Lord, will receive his discipline: and they that will seek him early, shall find a blessing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that seeketh the law, shall be filled with it: and he that dealeth deceitfully, shall meet with a stumblingblock therein.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that fear the Lord, shall find just judgment, and shall kindle justice as a light.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A sinful man will flee reproof, and will find an excuse according to his will.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man of counsel will not neglect understanding, a strange and proud man will not dread fear:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Even after he hath done with fear without counsel, he shall be controlled by the things of his own seeking.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, do thou nothing without counsel, and thou shalt not repent when thou hast done.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go not in the way of ruin, and thou shalt not stumble against the stones: trust not thyself to a rugged way, lest thou set a stumblingblock to thy soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And beware of thy own children, and take heed of them of thy household.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In every work of thine regard thy soul in faith: for this is the keeping of the commandments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that believeth God, taketh heed to the commandments: and he that trusteth in him, shall fare never the worse.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: No evils shall happen to him that feareth the Lord, but in temptation God will keep him and deliver him from evils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise man hateth not the commandments and justices, and he shall not be dashed in pieces as a ship in a storm.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man of understanding is faithful to the law of God, and the law is faithful to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that cleareth up a question, shall prepare what to say, and so having prayed he shall be heard, and shall keep discipline, and then he shall answer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of a fool is as a wheel of a cart: and his thoughts are like a rolling axletree.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A friend that is a mocker, is like a stallion horse: he neigheth under every one that sitteth upon him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why doth one day excel another, and one light another, and one year another year, when all come of the sun?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By the knowledge of the Lord they were distinguished, the sun being made, and keeping his commandment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he ordered the seasons, and holidays of them, and in them they celebrated festivals at an hour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Some of them God made high and great days, and some of them he put in the number of ordinary days. And all men are from the ground, and out of the earth, from whence Adam was created.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With much knowledge the Lord hath divided them and diversified their ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Some of them hath he blessed, and exalted: and some of them hath he sanctified, and set near himself: and some of them hath he cursed and brought low, and turned them from their station.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the potter’s clay is in his hand, to fashion and order it:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All his ways are according to his ordering: so man is in the hand of him that made him, and he will render to him according to his judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Good is set against evil, and life against death: so also is the sinner against a just man. And so look upon all the works of the most High. Two and two, and one against another.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I awaked last of all, and as one that gathereth after the grapegatherers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the blessing of God I also have hoped: and as one that gathereth grapes, have I filled the winepress.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: See that I have not laboured for myself only, but for all that seek discipline.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear me, ye great men, and all ye people, and hearken with your ears, ye rulers of the church.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give not to son or wife, brother or friend, power over thee while thou livest; and give not thy estate to another, lest thou repent, and thou entreat for the same.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As long as thou livest, and hast breath in thee, let no man change thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it is better that thy children should ask of thee, than that thou look toward the hands of thy children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In all thy works keep the pre-eminence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let no stain sully thy glory. In the time when thou shalt end the days of thy life, and in the time of thy decease, distribute thy inheritance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Fodder, and a wand, and a burden are for an ass: bread, and correction, and work for a slave.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He worketh under correction, and seeketh to rest: let his hands be idle, and he seeketh liberty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The yoke and the thong bend a stiff neck, and continual labours bow a slave.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Torture and fetters are for a malicious slave: send him to work, that he be not idle:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For idleness hath taught much evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Set him to work: for so it is fit for him. And if he be not obedient, bring him down with fetters, but be not excessive towards any one, and do no grievous thing without judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou have a faithful servant, let him be to thee as thy own soul: treat him as a brother: because in the blood of thy soul thou hast gotten him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou hurt him unjustly, he will run away:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if he rise up and depart, thou knowest not whom to ask, and in what way to seek him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The hopes of a man that is void of understanding are vain and deceitful: and dreams lift up fools.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The man that giveth heed to lying visions, is like to him that catcheth at a shadow, and followeth after the wind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The vision of dreams is the resemblance of one thing to another: as when a man’s likeness is before the face of a man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What can be made clean by the unclean? and what truth can come from that which is false?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Deceitful divinations and lying omens and the dreams of evildoers, are vanity:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the heart fancieth as that of a woman in travail: except it be a vision sent forth from the most High, set not thy heart upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For dreams have deceived many, and they have failed that put their trust in them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The word of the law shall be fulfilled without a lie, and wisdom shall be made plain in the mouth of the faithful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What doth he know, that hath not been tried? A man that hath much experience, shall think of many things: and he that hath learned many things, shall shew forth understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hath no experience, knoweth little: and he that hath been experienced in many things, multiplieth prudence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that hath not been tried, what manner of things doth he know? he that hath been surprised, shall abound with subtlety.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have seen many things by travelling, and many customs of things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sometimes I have been in danger of death for these things, and I have been delivered by the grace of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The spirit of those that fear God, is sought after, and by his regard shall be blessed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For their hope is on him that saveth them, and the eyes of God are upon them that love him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that feareth the Lord shall tremble at nothing, and shall not be afraid: for he is his hope.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The soul of him that feareth the Lord is blessed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To whom doth he look, and who is his strength?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The eyes of the Lord are upon them that fear him, he is their powerful protector, and strong stay, a defence from the heat, and a cover from the sun at noon,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A preservation from stumbling, and a help from falling: he raiseth up the soul, and enlighteneth the eyes, and giveth health, and life, and blessing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The offering of him that sacrificeth of a thing wrongfully gotten, is stained, and the mockeries of the unjust are not acceptable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord is only for them that wait upon him in the way of truth and justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The most High approveth not the gifts of the wicked: neither hath he respect to the oblations of the unjust, nor will he be pacified for sins by the multitude of their sacrifices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that offereth sacrifice of the goods of the poor, is as one that sacrificeth the son in the presence of his father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The bread of the needy, is the life of the poor: he that defraudeth them thereof, is a man of blood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that taketh away the bread gotten by sweat, is like him that killeth his neighbour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that sheddeth blood, and he that defraudeth the laborer of his hire, are brothers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When one buildeth up, and another pulleth down: what profit have they but the labour?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When one prayeth, and another curseth: whose voice will God hear?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that washeth himself after touching the dead, if he toucheth him again, what doth his washing avail?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So a man that fasteth for his sins, and doth the same again, what doth his humbling himself profit him? who will hear his prayer?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that keepeth the law, multiplieth offerings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is a wholesome sacrifice to take heed to the commandments, and to depart from all iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And to depart from injustice, is to offer a propitiatory sacrifice for injustices, and a begging of pardon for sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall return thanks, that offereth fine flour: and he that doth mercy, offereth sacrifice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To depart from iniquity is that which pleaseth the Lord, and to depart from injustice, is an entreaty for sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt not appear empty in the sight of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For all these things are to be done because of the commandment of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The oblation of the just maketh the altar fat, and is an odour of sweetness in the sight of the most High.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sacrifice of the just is acceptable, and the Lord will not forget the memorial thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give glory to God with a good heart: and diminish not the firstfruits of thy hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In every gift shew a cheerful countenance, and sanctify thy tithes with joy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give to the most High according to what he hath given to thee, and with a good eye do according to the ability of thy hands:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the Lord maketh recompense, and will give thee seven times as much.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do not offer wicked gifts, for such he will not receive.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And look not upon an unjust sacrifice, for the Lord is judge, and there is not with him respect of person.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord will not accept any person against a poor man, and he will hear the prayer of him that is wronged.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He will not despise the prayers of the fatherless: nor the widow, when she poureth out her complaint.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do not the widow’s tears run down the cheek, and her cry against him that causeth them to fall?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For from the cheek they go up even to heaven, and the Lord that heareth will not be delighted with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that adoreth God with joy, shall be accepted, and his prayer shall approach even to the clouds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The prayer of him that humbleth himself, shall pierce the clouds: and till it come nigh he will not be comforted: and he will not depart till the most High behold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord will not be slack, but will judge for the just, and will do judgment: and the Almighty will not have patience with them, that he may crush their back:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he will repay vengeance to the Gentiles, till he have taken away the multitude of the proud, and broken the sceptres of the unjust,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Till he have rendered to men according to their deeds: and according to the works of Adam, and according to his presumption,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Till he have judged the cause of his people, and he shall delight the just with his mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mercy of God is beautiful in the time of affliction, as a cloud of rain in the time of drought.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Have mercy upon us, O God of all, and behold us, and shew us the light of thy mercies:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And send thy fear upon the nations, that have not sought after thee: that they may know that there is no God beside thee, and that they may shew forth thy wonders.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lift up thy hand over the strange nations, that they may see thy power.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as thou hast been sanctified in us in their sight, so thou shalt be magnified among them in our presence,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That they may know thee, as we also have known thee, that there is no God beside thee, O Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Renew thy signs, and work new miracles.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Glorify thy hand, and thy right arm.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Raise up indignation, and pour out wrath.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take away the adversary, and crush the enemy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hasten the time, and remember the end, that they may declare thy wonderful works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let him that escapeth be consumed by the rage of the fire: and let them perish that oppress thy people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Crush the head of the princes of the enemies that say: There is no other beside us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Gather together all the tribes of Jacob: that they may know that there no God besides thee, and may declare thy great works: and thou shalt inherit them as from the beginning.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Have mercy on thy people, upon whom thy name is invoked: and upon Israel, whom thou hast raised up to be thy firstborn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Have mercy on Jerusalem, the city which thou hast sanctified, the city of thy rest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Fill Sion with thy unspeakable words, and thy people with thy glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give testimony to them that are thy creatures from the beginning, and raise up the prophecies which the former prophets spoke in thy name.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Reward them that patiently wait for thee, that thy prophets may be found faithful: and hear the prayers of thy servants,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: According to the blessing of Aaron over thy people, and direct us into the way of justice, and let all know that dwell upon the earth, that thou art God the beholder of all ages.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The belly will devour all meat, yet one is better than another.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The palate tasteth venison and the wise heart false speeches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A perverse heart will cause grief, and a man of experience will resist it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A woman will receive every man: yet one daughter is better than another.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The beauty of a woman cheereth the countenance of her husband, and a man desireth nothing more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If she have a tongue that can cure, and likewise mitigate and shew mercy: her husband is not like other men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that possesseth a good wife, beginneth a possession: she is a help like to himself, and a pillar of rest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where there is no hedge, the possession shall be spoiled: and where there is no wife, he mourneth that is in want.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who will trust him that hath no rest, and that lodgeth wheresoever the night taketh him, as a robber well appointed, that skippeth from city to city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Every friend will say: I also am his friend: but there is a friend, that is only a friend in name. Is not this a grief even to death?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But a companion and a friend shall be turned to an enemy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O wicked presumption, whence camest thou to cover the earth with thy malice, and deceitfulness?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a companion who rejoiceth with his friend in his joys, but in the time of trouble, he will be against him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a companion who condoleth with his friend for his belly’s sake, and he will take up a shield against the enemy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forget not thy friend in thy mind, and be not unmindful of him in thy riches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Consult not with him that layeth a snare for thee, and hide thy counsel from them that envy thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Every counsellor giveth out counsel, but there is one that is a counsellor for himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Beware of a counsellor. And know before what need he hath: for he will devise to his own mind:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lest he thrust a stake into the ground, and say to thee:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy way is good; and then stand on the other side to see what shall befall thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Treat not with a man without religion concerning holiness, nor with an unjust man concerning justice, nor with a woman touching her of whom she is jealous, nor with a coward concerning war, nor with a merchant about traffic, nor with a buyer of selling, nor with an envious man of giving thanks,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nor with the ungodly of piety, nor with the dishonest of honesty, nor with the field laborer of every work,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nor with him that worketh by the year of the finishing of the year, nor with an idle servant of much business: give no heed to these in any matter of counsel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But be continually with a holy man, whomsoever thou shalt know to observe the fear of God,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whose soul is according to thy own soul: and who, when thou shalt stumble in the dark, will be sorry for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And establish within thyself a heart of good counsel: for there is no other thing of more worth to thee than it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The soul of a holy man discovereth sometimes true things, more than seven watchmen that sit in a high place to watch.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But above all these things pray to the most High, that he may direct thy way in truth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In all thy works let the true word go before thee, and steady counsel before every action.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wicked word shall change the heart: out of which four manner of things arise, good and evil, life and death: and the tongue is continually the ruler of them. There is a man that is subtle and a teacher of many, and yet is unprofitable to his own soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A skilful man hath taught many, and is sweet to his own soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that speaketh sophistically, is hateful: he shall be destitute of every thing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Grace is not given him from the Lord: for he is deprived of all wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a wise man that is wise to his own soul: and the fruit of his understanding is commendable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise man instructeth his own people, and the fruits of his understanding are faithful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise man shall be filled with blessings, and they that see shall praise him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The life of a man is in the number of his days: but the days of Israel are innumerable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A wise man shall inherit honour among his people, and his name shall live for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, prove thy soul in thy life: and if it be wicked, give it no power:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For all things are not expedient for all, and every kind pleaseth not every soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not greedy in any feasting, and pour not out thyself upon any meat:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For in many meats there will be sickness, and greediness will turn to choler.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By surfeiting many have perished, but he that is temperate, shall prolong life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Honour the physician for the need thou hast of him: for the most High hath created him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For all healing is from God, and he shall receive gifts of the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The skill of the physician shall lift up his head, and in the sight of great men he shall be praised.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The most High hath created medicines out of the earth, and a wise man will not abhor them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Was not bitter water made sweet with wood?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The virtue of these things is come to the knowledge of men, and the most High hath given knowledge to men, that he may be honoured in his wonders.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By these he shall cure and shall allay their pains, and of these the apothecary shall make sweet confections, and shall make up ointments of health, and of his works there shall be no end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the peace of God is over all the face of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, in thy sickness neglect not thyself, but pray to the Lord, and he shall heal thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Turn away from sin and order thy hands aright, and cleanse thy heart from all offence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give a sweet savour, and a memorial of fine flour, and make a fat offering, and then give place to the physician.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the Lord created him: and let him not depart from thee, for his works are necessary.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For there is a time when thou must fall into their hands:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall beseech the Lord, that he would prosper what they give for ease and remedy, for their conversation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that sinneth in the sight of his Maker, shall fall into the hands of the physician.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My son, shed tears over the dead, and begin to lament as if thou hadst suffered some great harm, and according to judgment cover his body, and neglect not his burial.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And for fear of being ill spoken of weep bitterly for a day, and then comfort thyself in thy sadness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And make mourning for him according to his merit for a day, or two, for fear of detraction.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For of sadness cometh death, and it overwhelmeth the strength, and the sorrow of the heart boweth down the neck.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In withdrawing aside sorrow remaineth: and the substance of the poor is according to his heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give not up thy heart to sadness, but drive it from thee: and remember the latter end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forget it not: for there is no returning, and thou shalt do him no good, and shalt hurt thyself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember my judgment: for thine also shall be so: yesterday for me, and to day for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When the dead is at rest, let his remembrance rest, and comfort him in the departing of his spirit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wisdom of a scribe cometh by his time of leisure: and he that is less in action, shall receive wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With what wisdom shall he be furnished that holdeth the plough, and that glorieth in the goad, that driveth the oxen therewith, and is occupied in their labours, and his whole talk is about the offspring of bulls?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall give his mind to turn up furrows, and his care is to give the kine fodder.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So every craftsman and workmaster that laboureth night and day, he who maketh graven seals, and by his continual diligence varieth the figure: he shall give his mind to the resemblance of the picture, and by his watching shall finish the work.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So doth the smith sitting by the anvil and considering the iron work. The vapour of the fire wasteth his flesh, and he fighteth with the heat of the furnace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The noise of the hammer is always in his ears, and his eye is upon the pattern of the vessel he maketh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He setteth his mind to finish his work, and his watching to polish them to perfection.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So doth the potter sitting at his work, turning the wheel about with his feet, who is always carefully set to his work, and maketh all his work by number:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He fashioneth the clay with his arm, and boweth down his strength before his feet:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall give his mind to finish the glazing, and his watching to make clean the furnace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All these trust to their hands, and every one is wise in his own art.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Without these a city is not built.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall not dwell, nor walk about therein, and they shall not go up into the assembly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Upon the judges’ seat they shall not sit, and the ordinance of judgment they shall not understand, neither shall they declare discipline and judgment, and they shall not be found where parables are spoken:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they shall strengthen the state of the world, and their prayer shall be in the work of their craft, applying their soul, and searching in the law of the most High.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wise man will seek out the wisdom of all the ancients, and will be occupied in the prophets.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He will keep the sayings of renowned men, and will enter withal into the subtilties of parables.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He will search out the hidden meanings of proverbs, and will be conversant in the secrets of parables.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall serve among great men, and appear before the governor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall pass into strange countries: for he shall try good and evil among men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He will give his heart to resort early to the Lord that made him, and he will pray in the sight of the most High.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He will open his mouth in prayer, and will make supplication for his sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For if it shall please the great Lord, he will fill him with the spirit of understanding:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he will pour forth the words of his wisdom as showers, and in his prayer he will confess to the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he shall direct his counsel, and his knowledge, and in his secrets shall he meditate.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shall shew forth the discipline he hath learned, and shall glory in the law of the covenant of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many shall praise his wisdom, and it shall never be forgotten.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The memory of him shall not depart away, and his name shall be in request from generation to generation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nations shall declare his wisdom, and the church shall shew forth his praise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If he continue, he shall leave a name above a thousand: and if he rest, it shall be to his advantage.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will yet meditate that I may declare: for I am filled as with a holy transport.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By a voice he saith: Hear me, ye divine offspring, and bud forth as the rose planted by the brooks of waters.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give ye a sweet odour as frankincense.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Send forth flowers, as the lily, and yield a smell, and bring forth leaves in grace, and praise with canticles, and bless the Lord in his works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Magnify his name, and give glory to him with the voice of your lips, and with the canticles of your mouths, and with harps, and in praising him, you shall say in this manner:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All the works of the Lord are exceeding good.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At his word the waters stood as a heap: and at the words of his mouth the receptacles of waters:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For at his commandment favour is shewn, and there is no diminishing of his salvation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The works of all flesh are before him, and there is nothing hid from his eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He seeth from eternity to eternity, and there is nothing wonderful before him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is no saying: What is this, or what is that? for all things shall be sought in their time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His blessing hath overflowed like a river.
